title: Architecture Patterns
subtitle: Monolith, microservices, MVC, and event-driven architectures

sections.title: Monolith vs Microservices
sections.text:
Monolith — Everything in one big app. One codebase, one deployment, one database. Simple to build, test, and deploy. Perfect for startups and small teams. The only problem: when it grows, everything is tangled together.

Microservices — Each feature is its own mini-app with its own API, database, and deployment. Services talk over the network. Scales independently but adds massive complexity: network issues, data consistency, monitoring, debugging across services.

The rule: Start with a monolith. Only extract services when you have a clear reason. Premature microservices are the #1 architecture mistake juniors make.
sections.caption: Monolith vs Microservices — different tradeoffs for different team sizes
sections.callout: Start with a monolith. Extract to microservices when you have clear bounded contexts AND the team size to maintain them. Premature microservices add complexity without benefit.

sections.title: MVC — Model-View-Controller
sections.text:
MVC is the most common pattern in web frameworks (Rails, Django, Laravel, Spring). It splits your app into three parts:

  • Model — Your data and business rules. Represents database tables, handles validation and queries.
  • View — What the user sees. HTML templates or JSON responses.
  • Controller — The middleman. Receives requests, calls the right Model code, and returns the right View.

Think of it like a restaurant: the Controller is the waiter (takes orders, serves food), the Model is the recipe book (knows how things work), and the View is the plate (how it looks to the customer). Keep controllers thin — they should just connect Models to Views, nothing more.
sections.caption: MVC: Controller coordinates between Model (data) and View (presentation)

sections.title: Event-Driven Architecture
sections.text:
Instead of services calling each other directly, they publish events to a message queue. Other services subscribe to events they care about. When an order is placed, the Order Service doesn't need to know about the Email Service, Inventory Service, or Analytics Service — it just publishes order.placed and everyone who cares picks it up.

RabbitMQ is great for complex routing and RPC. Kafka is built for high-throughput event streaming with replay capability. Both let services be decoupled — if the Email Service is down, events wait in the queue until it recovers.

This pattern shines when many services need to react to the same events. It's complex but gives you resilience and independent scaling.
sections.caption: Event-driven: one event (order.placed) triggers multiple independent services
sections.detail: Event-driven architecture's key advantage is temporal decoupling — services don't need to be running at the same time. If the email service is down, the order event stays in the queue. When it comes back up, it processes the backlog. This improves resilience and allows each service to scale independently based on its own load.
